title: Hand-Colored Photographs from 19th Century Japan: 110 Images Capture the Waning Days of Traditional Japanese Society
description:
in History, Photography |   	May 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
What we euphemistiôÙcalôÙly refer to as the ãOpenôÙing of Japanã catôÙalyzed a periôÙo
body:
in History, Photography |   	May 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
What we euphemistiôÙcalôÙly refer to as the ãOpenôÙing of Japanã catôÙalyzed a periôÙod of seisôÙmic upheaval for the proud forôÙmerôÙly closed counôÙtry. Between the fall of the TokuôÙgawa shoguôÙnate in 1853 and the MeiôÙji restoraôÙtion in 1868, JapanôÙese sociôÙety changed rapidôÙly due to the sudôÙden forced influx of forôÙeign capôÙiôÙtal and influôÙence, much of it destrucôÙtive. ãUnemôÙployôÙment rose,ã writes hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan John W. DowôÙer, ãDomesôÙtic prices soared sky highãÎ. Much of Japan was wracked by famine in the mid 1860sãÎ. As if all this were not curse enough, the forôÙeignôÙers also brought cholera with them.ã They also brought phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy, and both WestôÙern and JapanôÙese phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphers docôÙuôÙmentôÙed not only the countryãs proôÙfound transôÙforôÙmaôÙtion, but also its traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal dress and culôÙture.

Closed for 200 years, Japan became a source of endôÙless fasôÙciôÙnaôÙtion for WestôÙernôÙers as artiôÙfacts made their way across the sea. Among them was ãan extenôÙsive phoôÙtoôÙgraphôÙic docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion of Japan,ã notes the New York PubôÙlic Library, and ãof interôÙacôÙtion between the JapanôÙese and forôÙeignôÙersã (ComôÙmodore Perryãs expeôÙdiôÙtion to Tokyo Bay includôÙed a daguerreoôÙtype phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphôÙer.)
ãIn the broadôÙest sense, phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy entered Asia from Europe and AmerôÙiôÙca as part of the process of coloôÙnialôÙism, but soon took root in those regions with local phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphers.ã

The colôÙorized images you see here come from the NYPLãs large colôÙlecôÙtion of late 19th cenôÙtuôÙry JapanôÙese phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy, takôÙen by phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphers like the ItalôÙian-British Felice Beato and his JapanôÙese stuôÙdent KimôÙbei, who ãassistôÙed Beato in the hand-colôÙorôÙing of phoôÙtographs until 1863,ã then ãset up his own large and flourôÙishôÙing stuôÙdio in YokoôÙhama in 1881.ã The archive proôÙvides ãa rich resource for the underôÙstandôÙing of the politôÙiôÙcal, social, ecoôÙnomôÙic, and artisôÙtic hisôÙtoôÙry of Asia from the 1870s to the earôÙly 20th cenôÙtuôÙry.ãô These images date from between 1890 and 1909, by which time much of Japan had already been extenôÙsiveôÙly westôÙernôÙized in dress, archiôÙtecôÙture, and style of govôÙernôÙment.

To many JapanôÙese, the old ways, susôÙtained through a couôÙple hunôÙdred years of isoôÙlaôÙtion, must have seemed in danôÙger of slipôÙping away. To many WestôÙernôÙers, howôÙevôÙer, the encounter with Japan offered a kind of culôÙturôÙal renewôÙal.ô As the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan MuseôÙum of Art points out, ãa tidal wave of forôÙeign importsã from Asia, includôÙing ãwoodôÙcut prints by masôÙters of the ukiyoãe schoolãÎ transôÙformed ImpresôÙsionôÙist and Post-ImpresôÙsionôÙist art.ã EuroôÙpean colôÙlecôÙtors, traders, and artists disôÙcovôÙered a mania for all things JapanôÙese, even as some of its culôÙturôÙal forms threatôÙened to disôÙapôÙpear. Enter the NYPLãs digôÙiôÙtal colôÙlecôÙtion, PhoôÙtographs of Japan, here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Behold the MasôÙterôÙpiece by Japanãs Last Great WoodôÙblock Artist: View Online TsukioôÙka Yoshitoshiãs One HunôÙdred Aspects of the Moon (1885)
What HapôÙpens When a JapanôÙese WoodôÙblock Artist Depicts Life in LonôÙdon in 1866, Despite NevôÙer HavôÙing Set Foot There
JapanôÙese KabuôÙki Actors CapôÙtured in 18th-CenôÙtuôÙry WoodôÙblock Prints by the MysôÙteôÙriôÙous & MasôÙterôÙful Artist Sharaku
The EvoôÙluôÙtion ofô The Great Wave off KanaôÙgawa: See Four VerôÙsions That HokuôÙsai PaintôÙed Over NearôÙly 40 Years
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Lost Scenes of Orson Wellesã The Magnificent Ambersons Are Being Controversially Restored with AI
description:
in Artificial Intelligence, Film, Technology |   	May 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When teleôÙviôÙsion mogul Ted TurnôÙer died earôÙliôÙer this
body:
in Artificial Intelligence, Film, Technology |   	May 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When teleôÙviôÙsion mogul Ted TurnôÙer died earôÙliôÙer this month, it gave cinephiles occaôÙsion to rememôÙber hisô brief but high-proôÙfile forôÙay into colôÙorizaôÙtion. In the mid-nineôÙteen-eightôÙies, he comôÙmisôÙsioned for broadôÙcast colôÙorized verôÙsions of more than 100 clasôÙsic movies, from The TreaôÙsure of the SierôÙra Madre to Itãs a WonôÙderôÙful Life to CasablanôÙca. It was only thanks to a clause specôÙiôÙfyôÙing a black-and-white picôÙtureô in Orson Wellesã conôÙtract with RKOô thatô CitôÙiôÙzen Kaneô nevôÙer got the full TurnôÙer treatôÙment. That blessôÙedôÙly failed project is now being invoked again in comôÙparôÙiôÙson with the startôÙup Fable StuôÙdioãs enterôÙprise, underôÙway even now, of using artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence to restore Wellesã sophoôÙmore feaôÙture The MagôÙnifôÙiôÙcent AmberôÙsons, which was notoôÙriôÙousôÙly mutiôÙlatôÙed by the stuôÙdio before its release in 1942.
The recut hapôÙpened in Wellesã absence. After the attack on Pearl HarôÙbor, he received what sounds like someôÙthing more than a request from NelôÙson RockôÙeôÙfeller, then the governmentãs CoorôÙdiôÙnaôÙtor of Inter-AmerôÙiôÙcan Affairs, to go to Brazil and shoot a docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary about CarôÙniôÙval in the interôÙest of ãPan-AmerôÙiôÙcan uniôÙty.ã Due to a disôÙasôÙtrous test screenôÙing, as Welles explains in the clip from a 1982ô AreôÙna broadôÙcast above, ãit was thought by everyôÙone in HolôÙlyôÙwood, while I was in South AmerôÙiôÙca, that it was too ãdownôÙbeat,ã a famous HolôÙlyôÙwood word at the time.ã Yet the entire film, to his mind, was about the downôÙfall of the titôÙuôÙlar famôÙiôÙly, who lose their wealth and presôÙtige as the sociôÙety they knew slips out from underôÙneath them durôÙing the transôÙforôÙmaôÙtions of the earôÙly autoôÙmoôÙbile age: not a wideôÙly resôÙoôÙnant theme, it seems, in mid-twenôÙtiôÙeth-cenôÙtuôÙry AmerôÙiôÙca.
ãThey destroyedô AmberôÙsons,ã Welles says of the RKOãs recut, ãand the picôÙture itself destroyed me.ã Yet even the BowdôÙlerôÙized verôÙsion has more than a few admirôÙers. Among them is Edward Saatchi, the movie-lovôÙing adverôÙtisôÙing-comôÙpaôÙny scion behind this AI restoraôÙtion and/or reconôÙstrucôÙtion project. ãHis AmaôÙzon-backed generativeãA.I. platôÙform, ShowrunôÙner, would feed off the data from the extant verôÙsion of the film to prompt entire new scenes, based on voluôÙmiôÙnous proôÙducôÙtion mateôÙriôÙals that surôÙvived, includôÙing scripts, phoôÙtographs, and detailed notes,ã writes theô New YorkôÙerãs Michael SchulôÙman. ãFor emoôÙtionôÙal authenôÙticôÙiôÙty, Fable would first shoot live actors, then overôÙlay the footage with the digôÙiôÙtized voicôÙes and likeôÙnessôÙes of the long-dead cast memôÙbers.ã The result has the potenôÙtial to beô unsetôÙtling on sevôÙerôÙal levôÙels at once.
As SchulôÙman emphaôÙsizes, the filmãs conôÙcern with the human cost of a techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal revôÙoôÙluôÙtion is hardôÙly lost on Saatchi. ãWith all their speed forôÙward, they may be a step backôÙward in civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion,ã says Joseph CotôÙtenãs charôÙacôÙter, an earôÙly autoôÙmoôÙbile investor, in a scene from the stuôÙdio cut. ãIt may be that they wonãt add to the beauôÙty of the world or the life of menãs souls ã Iãm not sure. But autoôÙmoôÙbiles have come, and almost all outôÙward things are going to be difôÙferôÙent because of what they bring.ã Even the human mind, he specôÙuôÙlates, will be ãchanged in subôÙtle ways,ã a process clearôÙly in effect by the forôÙties. As far as the conôÙseôÙquences of AI, we can already see how itãs begun changôÙing the thinkôÙing of its earôÙly adopters. Saatchi himôÙself disôÙplays an ambivaôÙlence about the techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy, describôÙing it as ãpotenôÙtialôÙly the end of human creôÙativôÙiôÙtyã but also going full-speed-ahead with his unauôÙthoôÙrized work on The MagôÙnifôÙiôÙcent AmberôÙsons ã which, at the very least, heãs keepôÙing in black-and-white.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

AI ãComôÙpletesã KeiôÙth Haringãs UnfinôÙished PaintôÙing and ConôÙtroôÙverôÙsy Erupts
ArtiôÙfiôÙcial IntelôÙliôÙgence Brings to Life FigôÙures from 7 Famous PaintôÙings: The Mona Lisa, Birth of Venus & More
DisôÙcovôÙer the Lost Films of Orson Welles

When Ted TurnôÙer Tried to ColôÙorize CitôÙiôÙzen Kane: See the Only SurôÙvivôÙing Scene from the Great Act of CinôÙeôÙmatôÙic SacôÙriôÙlege
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

body:

title: Every Book of the Bible Explained in One Video
description:
in Books, History, Religion |   	May 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Whether weãre reliôÙgious or not, we can all agree that the Bible isnãt ju
body:
in Books, History, Religion |   	May 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Whether weãre reliôÙgious or not, we can all agree that the Bible isnãt just a book. In fact, itãs at least 66 of them, 39 Old TesôÙtaôÙment and 27 in the New, and thatãs just in the ProtesôÙtant traôÙdiôÙtion. Even if youãve nevôÙer read a sinôÙgle page of the Bible, you may well have a decent idea of what quite a few of those books conôÙtain: the stoôÙries of Adam, Eve, Noah, and the creôÙation in GenôÙeôÙsis; the plagues and Moses partôÙing the Red Sea in ExoôÙdus; the varôÙiôÙous depicôÙtions of Jesus in the Gospels that define his popôÙuôÙlar image; the apocôÙaôÙlypôÙtic grotesôÙqueries of RevôÙeôÙlaôÙtion. Thatãs even likeôÙliôÙer to be true if you watch HochelaôÙga, the YouTube chanôÙnel that just came out with a new video explainôÙing all those stoôÙries and everyôÙthing in between.
The result is long, to be sure, but not as long as you might expect: HochelaôÙga creôÙator TomôÙmie Trelawny manôÙages to covôÙer the 66 books of the Bible in two hours, the length of an ordiôÙnary feaôÙture film. For visuôÙals, he draws upon the hisôÙtoôÙry of WestôÙern art, whose conôÙnecôÙtions with ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty and penôÙchant for depictôÙing the reliôÙgionãs cenôÙtral events goes withôÙout sayôÙing.
In the case of bibôÙliôÙcal figôÙures like JonôÙah, Job, or Lotãs wife (before or after her conôÙverôÙsion into a pilôÙlar of salt), weãve develôÙoped our own menôÙtal images at least through culôÙturôÙal osmoôÙsis, informed or not by the visions of RenaisôÙsance masôÙters. But how many of us can call so easôÙiôÙly scenes from the books of ObaôÙdiôÙah, HagôÙgai, or PhileôÙmon up in our mindãs eye?
This video may prove most helpôÙful in proôÙvidôÙing a ãbig picôÙtureã of the Bible, allowôÙing viewôÙers with no expeôÙriôÙence of bibôÙliôÙcal scholôÙarôÙship to place isoôÙlatôÙed episodes to which theyãve heard refôÙerôÙences all their lives in conôÙtext with each othôÙer. And yet, itãs also entireôÙly posôÙsiôÙble that theyãll come out of these two hours wonôÙderôÙing to what extent all these parts realôÙly fit togethôÙer in the first place. ColôÙlectôÙed from mateôÙrôÙiôÙal origôÙiôÙnalôÙly writôÙten over cenôÙturies and in varôÙiôÙous forms, not to menôÙtion passed through the vagaries of transôÙlaôÙtion,ô the Bible could hardôÙly be expectôÙed to present itself with polôÙished coherôÙence.ô Whether or not you believe it conôÙtains the word of God, you could well feel ready, after HochelaôÙgaãs overview, to grapôÙple with its text in all its linôÙguisôÙtic richôÙness, its surôÙprisôÙing conôÙtraôÙdicôÙtions, and its moralô grandeur ã as well as its more-than-occaôÙsionôÙal strangeôÙness.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

What HapôÙpened to Jesusã Twelve DisôÙciôÙples After the Bible ã It Wasnãt PretôÙty

Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Gaia Sleeps Amid Sarah Eberle's Award-Winning Garden at the RHS Chelsea Flower Show
description: Nestled amid plants native to the U.K., a giant figure of Gaia, or Mother Nature, sleeps in a verdant garden. With willow-branch locks shaped by a
body:
Nestled amid plants native to the U.K., a giant figure of Gaia, or Mother Nature, sleeps in a verdant garden. With willow-branch locks shaped by artist Tom Hare and a crown of leaves, the figureãs face and shoulders are made from a fallen mature tree carved by Tim Wood. A winding pathway leads beneath an arch that extends the characterãs torso, created in the tradition of dry stone walls and meticulously assembled by the family-run outfit Noble Stonework.
Youãll find Gaia in a garden titled ãOn the Edgeã at this yearãs RHS Chelsea Flower Show, which has taken the top prize of Garden of the Year. The project is a collaboration between designer Sarah Eberle and Campaign to Protect Rural England (CPRE), which champions the countryside and the sustainable practices necessary to protect and preserve its ecosystems.
Photo by Neil Hepworth/RHS
For this yearãs exhibit, Eberle emphasized ãedgelands,ã or spaces between rural and urbanized areas like the borders of fields or even residential gardens. Think roadside berms or the seemingly unruly growth beside a canal. Often, these spots just look like a lot of weeds. Eberle sees not only the beauty, but the value, in these overlooked areas.
ãThese spaces connect millions of people to nature in everyday life, yet theyãre undervalued and under constant pressure,ã CPRE says in a statement. ãThis garden is an invitation to see them differently: not as ãleftoverã land, but as living places that can recover and thrive with the right care.ã Eberleãs choice of plants has a slightly wild aesthetic, with vines taking over the stone archãredolent of the U.K.ãs historic stone bridgesãand a graceful yet somehow satisfyingly chaotic arrangement of plants we might associate with untamed overgrowth.
The gardenãs design encourages people to consider using natural materials, cultivating local plants to help pollinators, and embracing ãflawsã like old stumps or rocky areas that can be havens for wildlife. Amid natureãs innate rhythms, Gaia is a gentle protector who snoozes calmly with everything in balance. Eberele describes the effect: ãA sense of abundance, a landscape under repair, the beauty in the ordinary. Itãs about how it makes you feelãitãs almost a homecoming, an embrace, a hug.ã
The Chelsea Flower Show is the flagship event of the Royal Horticultural Society, and it has been held on the grounds of the Royal Hospital since 1913, with the exception of a few skipped seasons during the two World Wars and in 2020. Itãs not just limited to British gardeners, however: exhibitors from around the globe conceive of some of the most creative gardens imaginable. Tickets are available on the RHS website, and the show continues through May 23 in London.
Photo ôˋ Mark Spencer
Photo ôˋ Mark Spencer
Photo ôˋ Mark Spencer

body:

May 18, 2026

Kate Mothes

In a distraction-free ãdream lab,ã Zendaya has carte blanche to create a new wardrobe in a short, stop-motion film written and directed by Spike Jonze. The advertisement, which announces a clothing line the actor co-created with apparel brand On, merges dance and playful optics as she maneuvers through some otherworldly trial and error.
You might also enjoy Jonzeãs mind-melting dance video for Apple featuring FKA twigs.

title: From Two Tons of Celadon, Jean Shin Sculpts a Metaphor for the Korean Diaspora
description: Incorporating nearly two tons of porcelain fragments, a monumental pair of vessels spills out into a pool of lustrous green. Shards of broken cups
body:
Incorporating nearly two tons of porcelain fragments, a monumental pair of vessels spills out into a pool of lustrous green. Shards of broken cups and saucers, pots, and other voluptuous forms blanket the gallery of the Green-House at Green-Wood for a new installation by Jean Shin.
Celadon Landscape is one of the latest projects in which the artist transforms a singular material into a sprawling sculpture. Found objects that bear traces of their former purposes and users are prized possessions in Shinãs New York studio, as these often-discarded items are nested into dynamic works that consider the relationship between consumption, environmental care, and community.

Green-Wood presents the second iteration of Celadon Landscape, which originated during the artistãs visits with ceramicists and makers in South Korea. Celadon production has a lengthy history in the region and dates back to at least the 10th century. As Shin encountered the heaps of imperfect pieces these artisans had cast aside, she found the pale green-blue material an apt metaphor for belonging, repair, and the diaspora. 
ãCeladon vases occupy a prized place in Korean cultural historyãobjects of reverence, painstakingly made and carefully preserved,ã the artist says. ãIn Celadon Landscape, I shift the gaze to what is usually discarded: thousands of broken ceramic shards. I see in their imperfection not loss, but beautyãfragments that still pulse with the memory of Koreaãs enduring legacy.ã
With materials donated by studios in and near the city of Icheon, Shin conceived of two bulbous vessels cloaked in patterned, painted, stamped, and textured bits of pottery. Resting on their sides, the mosaic forms appear to emerge from the earth below, as if theyãve been uncovered in an archaeological dig. None of the vesselsãthe original pieces or the large-scale reconstructionsãis presented whole and unblemished, suggesting a fragmentation that doesnãt disappear but rather is made anew.
Fabricated by Miotto Mosaics Art Studios, Inc., Celadon Landscape is on view through January 17 in New York, where Shin is based. Keep up with her projects on Instagram.

schema.headline: From Two Tons of Celadon, Jean Shin Sculpts a Metaphor for the Korean Diaspora
